data.title: Kohl’s announces pricing of private offering of $360 million senior secured notes
data.description:
• Kohl’s (NYSE:KSS) Thursday announced the pricing of its private offering of $360 million aggregate principal amount of 10.000% senior secured notes due 2030.
  • The Notes will be guaranteed by Kohl’s subsidiaries.
  • Certain guarantees will be secured by, among other collateral, 11 distribution centers and e-commerce fulfillment facilities, which will be held in a newly-formed holding company.
  • The offering is expected to close on May 30.
  • Kohl’s (NYSE:KSS) intends to use the net proceeds from the sale of the Notes in a series of transactions resulting in the repayment of borrowings under its revolving credit facility.
  • Kohl’s expects to borrow under its revolving credit facility to repay all of its 4.25% notes due 2025 at maturity.

data.title: MEG Energy declares CAD 0.30 dividend

data.title: Senseonics announces pricing of $50 million public offering
data.description:
Senseonics (NYSE:SENS) Thursday announced the pricing of an underwritten public offering of 100,000,000 shares of common stock at a price to the public of $0.50 per share of common stock.

The gross proceeds to Senseonics (NYSE:SENS) from the offering are expected to be $50 million.

data.title: Beam Global signals return to growth in Q2 2025 while expanding international reach and product diversity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Beam Global (BEEM) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Lisa Potok, CFO, reported revenues of $6.3 million for Q1 2025, with a "significant rebalancing towards enterprise customers whereas 53% of our revenues were derived from commercial customers compared to only 16% in the same period in 2024." International customers comprised 25% of revenue, up from 11% in Q1 2024. Potok stated, "Our gross profits for Q1 of 2025 was $0.5 million or an 8% gross margin versus our gross profit of $1.5 million or 10% gross margin in Q1 of 2024." She highlighted a "non-cash negative impact of $1 million for depreciation and amortization of intangible assets" and disclosed a goodwill impairment charge of $10.8 million, citing decreased market capitalization.
  • Desmond Wheatley, President, CEO & Chairman, emphasized, "Sales of our flagship product EV ARC increased in the first quarter. We're navigating our way through a series of uncertainties in the U.S. market and while we're hitting speed bumps along the way, we now believe that we have the pieces in place to return to growth in this quarter and in future quarters." Wheatley also noted, "Our battery business is doing some of the most interesting and promising work it's ever done. Our international expansion strategy is gaining momentum and bearing fruit. We have sufficient cash and working capital to continue to operate the business into the future. We have no debt and no going concern."

Outlook

  • Wheatley stated, "We now believe that we have the pieces in place to return to growth in this and future quarters. We've adjusted our sales efforts to concentrate on non-federal prospects and we've taken advantage of the moves that we've been making over the last couple of years to grow our geographic footprint through our expansion into Europe." Management expects increased commercial and international sales to drive revenue growth, with ongoing efforts to diversify the customer base and product portfolio.

Financial Results

  • Potok reported a backlog of $6.3 million at the end of Q1 2025. Gross margin net of non-cash items was 21%, compared to 12% in Q1 2024. Operating expenses totaled $16 million, including a $10.8 million goodwill impairment. The Q1 2025 net loss was $15.5 million, with $12.5 million stemming from non-cash expenses. Excluding non-cash items, the net loss was $2.8 million. Cash balance at quarter end was $2.5 million, and net cash used for operating activities was $1.8 million. Wheatley described the goodwill impairment as "entirely GAAP accounting driven adjustments to our financials, which I stress again are non-cash and have no impact on the operational viability of the company or in my opinion, its true value."

Q&A

  • Ryan Pfingst, B. Riley Securities: Asked about product mix and margin progression as new products and international sales grow. Wheatley responded that new products such as BeamPatrol, BeamBike, and BeamSpot are gaining traction, and "we're going to have a lot more diversity. With that diversity will come a reduction in the risk from impacts from large customers, fluctuations in their orders, as we've seen with the federal government this time. But also with that diversity will come a lot more opportunity for growth." On margins, Wheatley noted, "Our EV ARC gross margins right now, the unit... they're up still up in the 40% and 50% range. Our gross margins net of non-cash, so non-GAAP gross margins, taking out the non-cash contributions from amortization of intangible assets, that sort of stuff, still north of 20%."
  • Noel Parks, Tuohy Brothers: Asked about manufacturing capacity in Europe and the impacts of climate-related events on the resiliency thesis. Wheatley explained, "In Europe, we have 250,000 square feet under roof. 5x more under roof right now, and then six acres of land that we can those on inexpensively and with a great deal of assistance and help from the local authorities." On resiliency, Wheatley said, "Resiliency is a very significant piece of our business... we're not an EV charging company. We have to make products, which enable EV charging companies to deploy their chargers without construction or electrical work. And very importantly, in a way that doesn't require extra capacity on the grid and also continues to operate during blackouts and brownouts."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on product mix, margin sustainability, manufacturing scalability, and the resilience of Beam’s offerings, demonstrating an inquisitive but neutral tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone, repeatedly stressing breadth of product development and market diversification. Phrases such as "I'm feeling good about our gross margin, particularly in light of the fact that we've had this low volume quarter and still maintained 8% GAAP gross profit, 20%-plus percent net of non-cash items" reflect optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains focused on overcoming U.S. government headwinds, but confidence is more pronounced regarding international growth and product diversification. Analyst tone remained consistently neutral, prioritizing near-term execution details.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s transition away from U.S. federal government dependence accelerated, with commercial and international revenues making up larger shares. Product portfolio diversification and international expansion were further emphasized, with new products and regions driving order growth.
  • Management’s language shifted from cautiously optimistic in Q4 2024 to highlighting resilience and readiness for growth in Q2 2025 and beyond.
  • Key metrics reflected a continued contraction in revenue compared to the prior year, but a strategic pivot toward enterprise and international customers, and sustained progress in gross margin improvements (net of non-cash items).
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on product rollout timelines, margin trends, and geographic strategy, with questions in Q1 2025 more centered on scalability and risk mitigation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "uncertainty in the U.S. government's zero emission vehicle strategy related to the Presidential Election" as a key factor in reduced revenue. The company described a challenging environment due to the loss of immediate U.S. federal government sales but highlighted diversification as a mitigation strategy.
  • Wheatley acknowledged ongoing risks related to tariffs and inflationary pressures on materials, but stressed, "those tariffs should be less impactful to us than to many others" due to the company’s U.S. manufacturing and international operations.
  • The $10.8 million goodwill impairment was attributed to stock price declines rather than operational issues, and management maintained that "the value of our acquisitions is greater today than it was when we made them."

Final Takeaway

Management signaled confidence that Beam Global has established the foundation needed for renewed growth, citing a diversified customer mix, international expansion, and a broadened product portfolio as key drivers for upcoming quarters. Despite lower revenues and a significant non-cash goodwill impairment, the company emphasized ongoing gross profit improvements, no debt, and a strategy designed to withstand U.S. federal spending volatility. Leadership highlighted their expectation for a return to growth beginning in Q2 2025, with international and commercial sales providing momentum for the remainder of the year and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Housing Starts and Permits 
 
Housing starts measure the initial construction of single-family and multi-family units on a monthly basis. Data on permits provide indications of future construction. 
Starts are expected marginally better at 1.362 million units in April versus 1.324 million in March.
 
8:30 AM Import and Export Prices 
 
Import price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods that are bought in the United States but produced abroad and export price indexes are compiled for the prices of goods sold abroad but produced domestically.
 
Imports and exports both expected down 0.3 percent on the month.
 
10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment 
 
The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.
 
After dropping another 8 percent in April, the index is expected to steady at its low levels, 53.0 for confidence. Inflation expectations are seen rising again to a remarkable 6.6% from 6.5% in April and 3.3 percent in May 2024.
 
1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count 
 
The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. Used for drilling wellbores for wells that may eventually produce oil or gas, active rigs are essential for the exploration and development of oil and gas fields. Rigs that are not active are not counted. 
 
4:00 PM Treasury International Capital 
 
These Treasury data track the flows of financial instruments into and out of the United States. Instruments tracked include Treasury securities, agency securities, corporate bonds, and corporate equities.
 
6:00 PM Fed's Barkin Speech 
 
Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin will give commencement address at Vance-Granville Community College.
 
8:40 PM Fed's Daly Speech 
 
San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will give commencement address at the College of Western Idaho.

data.title: TSS expects 50%+ year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth as AI rack demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TSS, Inc. (TSSI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • President and CEO Darryll Dewan opened by highlighting a strong start to 2025, stating demand for AI rack integration and procurement services "remains robust," and emphasizing the company's operational execution: "We are successfully executing our business strategy, delivering substantial growth in revenue, earnings and cash flow... while scaling our operations and positioning the company to capture a meaningful share of the rapidly growing and complex AI infrastructure market."
  • Dewan reported a milestone with the new 213,000 square foot Georgetown, Texas facility, which has begun production and is expected to reach full capacity by June. He described it as "a building designed from the ground up for AI rack integration and there are very few buildings like this in the market, providing us a significant competitive advantage."
  • Dewan detailed segment performance, noting procurement services revenue grew "by more than 600% to more than $90 million in the quarter" and systems integration revenue increased "more than 250%." Facilities management revenue declined 40% but Dewan anticipates a future role for modular data centers as AI adoption accelerates.
  • Dewan stated, "Based on our current forecast, we anticipate a payback period of approximately 2 years" on the Georgetown investment, with total planned investment between $25 million and $30 million.
  • CFO Daniel Chism said, "Consolidated revenue increased by more than 520% in the first quarter of 2025 to $99 million, up from $15.9 million in the first quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Dewan reaffirmed the company expects total revenue in the first half of 2025 will surpass the second half of 2024. He reiterated guidance that "full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA to be at least 50% higher than all of last year, driven by higher volume, improved operational leverage and strategic investments made over the past year."
  • Dewan cautioned about macro risks: "If the tariff situation does not stabilize, we and all others in the IT hardware supply chain will possibly see orders taking longer to process."

Financial Results

  • Chism reported procurement services revenue reached $90.2 million, up 676% year-over-year, and accounted for 77% of all procurement revenues in 2024.
  • Systems integration revenue rose to $7.5 million, up from $2.1 million in Q1 2024. Facilities management revenue was $1.3 million, down from $2.1 million.
  • Gross profit increased 674% to $7 million; consolidated gross margin was 9.3%, down from 17.1% due to a higher mix of lower-margin procurement revenue. Systems integration gross margin was 22% (28% prior year), but Chism clarified that excluding noncash rent, margins improved to 32%.
  • Operating income was $4.1 million, with a 4.2% margin; net income was $3 million, up from $15,000 in Q1 2024. Diluted EPS was $0.12, up from just above breakeven.
  • Cash and short-term deposits were $27.3 million as of March 31, 2025. Cash flow from operations was $20.6 million for Q1 2025.

Q&A

  • Kris Tuttle, Blue Caterpillar: Asked about long-term relevance of TSS integration services amid efforts by Dell and NVIDIA to simplify integration. Dewan responded, "We're trying to do the best we can to add value to integrate those solutions faster and with more value and quality than anybody else in the market... it's going to get more complex. We're told... it's going to continue to evolve."
  • Unidentified Analyst: Inquired about the old facility's future. Dewan said costs are built into the model and options include subleasing or expanding configuration services.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked if full capacity revenue for the new facility was disclosed. Dewan replied, "The answer is no." Chism reiterated EBITDA guidance.
  • Bradley Stevenson: Questioned margin pressure as volumes rise. Chism stated, "I do anticipate margins going up in the remainder of the year." He attributed lower Q1 margins to noncash rent expense and expects improvement.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked if high procurement revenue is sustainable. Dewan and Chism noted optimism but said volumes will fluctuate due to project-driven nature and federal seasonality.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Probed facilities management decline. Dewan said the segment is transitioning to support AI-driven modular deployments, with upside potential if new deals close.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about AI infrastructure demand durability. Dewan said demand visibility is strong for 90 days to 5 months, and Chism estimated enterprise investments last 5-6 years, with hyperscaler refreshes in 2-4 years.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about customer base diversification beyond Dell. Dewan emphasized growth opportunities while maintaining trusted relationships, considering expansion via acquisition, partnerships, or new services.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed strong enthusiasm and congratulated management on execution, but questions focused on sustainability, margin trends, and customer concentration, showing a slightly positive but probing tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently expressing pride in execution and future prospects. Dewan stated, "We're optimistic," and Chism provided detailed, transparent explanations of margin dynamics and growth drivers.
  • Compared to the prior quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased confidence, with analysts showing more overt praise but continuing to explore long-term risks and growth drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue and EPS accelerated sharply from Q4 2024, with Q1 2025 revenue at $99 million (Q4: $50 million) and EPS at $0.12 (Q4: $0.08).
  • Management's guidance language remained firm, reiterating full-year adjusted EBITDA growth of at least 50%.
  • Strategic focus shifted to operationalizing the new Georgetown facility, emphasizing production ramp and power infrastructure as a differentiator.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin sustainability, procurement revenue volatility, and growth beyond key customers, similar to the previous quarter but with greater attention to AI infrastructure durability and customer diversification.
  • Management's tone remained confident, while analysts became more positive but maintained scrutiny on long-term execution and risk.

Risks and Concerns

  • Dewan cited "uncertainty caused by trade and tariffs" as a risk, potentially impacting IT hardware costs and supply chains.
  • Procurement revenue subject to quarter-to-quarter fluctuations, especially due to federal government buying cycles.
  • Facilities management revenue declined, but management is optimistic about modular data center growth as AI adoption spreads.
  • Management is actively addressing talent acquisition and operational scaling to meet demand at new facilities.
  • Analysts probed on margin pressures, sustainability of procurement volumes, and customer concentration risks.

Final Takeaway

TSS management emphasized robust demand for AI-driven integration and procurement, record-breaking revenue and profitability, and the strategic advantage of the new Georgetown facility. With clear visibility into near-term demand and a focus on profitable growth, management reiterated its outlook for at least 50% higher adjusted EBITDA in 2025, while vigilantly monitoring macroeconomic and industry risks. The team remains committed to operational excellence and expanding its role in the evolving AI infrastructure market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Worksport targets $20M–$25M 2025 revenues as AL4 launch and dealer expansion drive growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Worksport Ltd. (WKSP) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Steven Rossi described Q1 2025 as a “transformative period” for Worksport, highlighting the release of the flagship AL4 tonneau cover, rapid dealer network expansion, and strengthened gross margins. Rossi indicated, “We released our flagship higher-margin products, the AL4 tonneau cover. We rapidly expanded our dealer network and strengthened our gross margins.”
  • Rossi stated, “Revenue for the quarter reached $2.24 million, a 337% increase compared to $512,000 in Q1 of last year. While this figure is slightly lower than our Q4 2024 results, this decrease is consistent with seasonal trends.” He emphasized strong year-over-year growth expectations for 2025.
  • Michael Johnston, CFO, reported, “Gross profit rose notably to $396,000, achieving a gross margin of nearly 18%, which is up from 7% in the same quarter last year and 11% in Q4 of 2024.” Johnston stated, “We expect margin to continue improving with an objective of reaching 25% to 30% later this year.”
  • Rossi highlighted the dealer network growth to 151 active dealers, a 64% increase from 92 at the end of last year, and noted, “March 2025 business-to-business sales grew nearly 70% from February.”
  • Worksport plans to launch three new products in 2025: the HD3 hard folding tonneau cover, the SOLIS solar integrated tonneau cover, and the COR portable energy storage system.

Outlook

  • Johnston forecasted full year 2025 revenues in the range of $20 million to $25 million, stating, “We are targeting full year 2025 revenues in the range of $20 million to $25 million.” He explained that Q2 revenue is expected to see further improvement due to the AL4 launch.
  • Management expects tonneau cover sales to ramp up significantly in Q3 and Q4, with an initial target of $2 million to $3 million in revenue from COR and SOLIS by year-end, although this may be updated as global supply chain conditions evolve.
  • Johnston stated, “We currently forecast gross margin reaching the 25% to 30% range or higher by late 2025.” Cash flow breakeven is targeted for Q4 2025 or early Q1 2026.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 2025 was $2.24 million, compared to $2.93 million in Q4 2024 and $512,000 in Q1 2024.
  • Gross profit for the quarter was $396,000, with a gross margin of nearly 18%.
  • Operating expenses for Q1 were $4.65 million, up from $3.68 million in Q1 2024. Sales and marketing expenses were $870,000, and general and administrative expenses rose to approximately $2.99 million. Professional fees decreased to $426,000.
  • Operating loss for Q1 2025 was approximately $4.46 million, with a net loss of $4.46 million.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $5.08 million, slightly up from $4.88 million at year-end 2024. Working capital improved to $7.94 million.
  • Inventories at quarter-end were $5.7 million, with $3.4 million in raw materials, primarily domestically sourced aluminum.

Q&A

  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim Group: Have you made sales of the AL4 product this quarter? Steven Rossi: “AL4, it's flying off the shelves. We can't keep up. We were actually running low on stock last week. We're not only selling it. We're selling the heck out of it. It's going extremely well.”
  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim Group: What are the raw materials within the inventory budget? Rossi explained that most inventory is American-sourced aluminum, accounting for 60% to 70% of the bill of materials for AL3, AL4, and HD3, with SOLIS including additional solar technology costs.
  • Scott Buck, H.C. Wainwright: What do you have in place for distribution with COR and SOLIS? Rossi shared plans to begin with direct-to-consumer sales, then move to distribution through the existing dealer network, citing “a very sincere amount of interest from like very large businesses with fleets of thousands of pickup trucks, oil and gas, exploration and obviously, through our connections as disclosed in our previous press releases relating to government services.”
  • Scott Buck, H.C. Wainwright: Are new products driving higher gross margins? Rossi indicated both scaling and premium positioning are contributing, noting, “We're going to raise our profitability metrics, but we're also going to increase the economies -- margins through economies of scale. So it's actually both top and bottom.”
  • C.K. Poe Fratt, Alliance Global Partners: Is the $20 million to $25 million guidance inclusive of SOLIS and COR? Rossi clarified that current guidance excludes SOLIS and COR sales, stating, “this quarter, we're removing the guidance of SOLIS and COR sales altogether, although we intend to sell them and we intend that there'll be a pleasant surprise in addition to the guidance that we've issued.”
  • Poe Fratt also confirmed that soft tonneau covers are being phased out and that production may shift to North America.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was inquisitive but focused on clarity around product sales, raw materials, margin drivers, and guidance inclusions. Questions sought to clarify drivers of guidance and margin improvement, with neutral to slightly positive sentiment.
  • Management was confident and enthusiastic, particularly when discussing AL4 sales and production scaling. Rossi’s repeated references to strong market response and operational momentum indicated optimism. Johnston emphasized “objective of reaching 25% to 30%” gross margins and targeting cash flow breakeven, signaling confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained positive, but management’s language showed more precision in what is and isn’t included in guidance, reflecting a careful approach amid supply chain and geopolitical uncertainties.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance narrowed to $20 million to $25 million for 2025, compared to the previous quarter’s range of $20 million to $34.5 million, with the current quarter’s guidance excluding SOLIS and COR sales due to supply chain uncertainty.
  • Dealer network growth accelerated, with a 64% increase in active dealers in Q1 2025 compared to a 30% expansion in the first two months of the previous year.
  • Gross margin improved to nearly 18% in Q1 from 11% in Q4 2024, and management reiterated targets for 25% to 30% by late 2025.
  • Management’s tone became more cautious regarding new product contributions to guidance, focusing only on tonneau covers for revenue outlook.
  • Analysts focused more on raw material sourcing, product launch timelines, and the specificity of revenue guidance, compared to broader market and profitability concerns in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing investments in inventory and production to support demand, while monitoring for slow-moving or obsolete stock.
  • Rossi addressed tariff risks, explaining that U.S. sourcing mitigates long-term impacts on core products.
  • Guidance for new product sales (SOLIS and COR) was excluded this quarter due to ongoing geopolitical and supply chain volatility, with management indicating willingness to update guidance as conditions improve.
  • Analysts questioned the inclusion of new product revenues and the company’s ability to scale production efficiently.

Final Takeaway

Worksport’s Q1 2025 earnings call emphasized strong year-over-year revenue growth, a successful AL4 launch, and a rapidly expanding dealer network. The company narrowed 2025 revenue guidance to $20 million to $25 million, focusing on tonneau covers and excluding new product lines from projections due to supply chain uncertainties. Management reported continued gross margin improvement and targeted breakeven by late 2025 or early 2026, with major product launches and scaling plans underpinning their optimism for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Advanced Drainage Systems outlines $2.825B–$2.975B FY26 revenue target as market diversification drives resilience
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Advanced Drainage Systems (WMS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Scott Barbour, President and CEO, reported net sales of $2.9 billion for fiscal 2025, representing a 1% increase over the prior year. He highlighted, "domestic construction market sales increased 3% as we continue to drive above-market performance through our material conversion strategy in the stormwater and on-site wastewater market." Barbour emphasized strong double-digit growth in Florida and Texas, particularly in pipe, Allied products, and Infiltrator products. He pointed out, "water quality products increased double digits as did the Cultec retention/detention chambers that we acquired in 2022. The tank and active treatment product in Infiltrator each grew double digits also."
  • Barbour described the company's strategic diversification over the past decade, stating, "We have strategically diversified the product, the geography and the end market mix to become a higher margin, more profitable business." He also explained the decision to postpone Investor Day due to current economic uncertainty, clarifying, "I didn't think that the audience is going to be the most receptive to a major Investor Day talking about how things look over the next three years... we are going to push the Investor Day and we'll be in touch with the new day that will be sometime later this year."
  • Craig Taylor, Executive Vice President and President of Infiltrator, reviewed Infiltrator’s fiscal 2025 performance: "We reported $560 million in sales, an increase of 15% over prior year, including $46 million in a record sales. On an organic basis, sales increased 5% driven by double-digit growth in both septic tanks and advanced treatment products." Taylor highlighted margin improvements and innovation, noting, "New products we have introduced in the past three years account for over 20% of our revenue."
  • CFO Scott Cottrill stated, "In the fourth quarter, net sales decreased 6% overall as demand was impacted by higher interest rates, economic uncertainty and unfavorable weather conditions." Cottrill further reported, "we generated $581 million of cash from operations during fiscal 2025... Capital spending increased 15% to $212 million in fiscal 2025." He announced a 13% increase in the annual dividend to $0.72 per share and the acquisition of River Valley Pipe, stating, "River Valley makes a lot of sense for us and we acquired a nice customer list in addition to a nice product line."

Outlook

  • Cottrill provided fiscal 2026 guidance: "we expect revenue to be in the range of $2.825 billion and $2.975 billion and adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $850 million to $910 million. These ranges result in an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.1% to 30.6%, down 50 basis points to flat compared to this year's 30.6% margin." Management does not anticipate material impact from tariffs and expects nonresidential and residential end markets to be pressured by higher interest rates and economic uncertainty.
  • Infrastructure market growth is expected in low single digits, while agriculture and international end markets are expected to be down double digits. Cottrill stated, "for revenue, volume up low digits and pricing down low single digits. For profitability, price/cost should be neutral for the year as we expect lower material costs year-over-year to offset the impact of pricing."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for fiscal 2025 were $2.9 billion, with adjusted EBITDA margin at 30.6%. Infiltrator reported $560 million in sales for the year. Free cash flow from operations reached $581 million. Capital spending rose to $212 million, and $121 million was returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Liquidity at year end stood at $1.1 billion and net leverage at 1.1 times. The annual dividend was increased to $0.72 per share.
  • Cottrill highlighted ongoing investment in customer service, technology, production capacity, and safety. Key growth investments included the expansion of recycling operations and upgrading transportation assets.

Q&A

  • Mike Halloran, Baird: Asked about sequential pricing pressure and share gain catalysts. Cottrill responded, "sequentially throughout the year relatively flat on price." Barbour added, "there are share gains through conversion from traditional materials to plastic pipe... HP or polypropylene products and primarily in the kind of what I would call the larger diameter."
  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays: Queried revenue cadence and Investor Day postponement. Cottrill indicated, "expect 55% to 60% of the revenue in the first half, consistent with what we normally generate and drive." Barbour explained postponement was due to lack of comfort in providing a three-year plan amid uncertainty.
  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Asked about order rates and Orenco integration. Barbour stated, "Order rates are trending positive and definitely support the guidance that we gave in the first half, second half." Taylor confirmed a target of 1,000 basis points margin expansion at Orenco over the next three to five years.
  • John Lovallo, UBS: Sought organic versus inorganic growth detail. Cottrill clarified, "organically at the midpoint, we're going to be flat on the volume side of the house."
  • Garik Shmois, Loop Capital: Inquired about pricing stability and capital allocation. Barbour emphasized daily pricing management and confidence in the process, while Cottrill affirmed readiness for further acquisitions and innovation investments.
  • Jeffrey Hammond, KeyBanc: Probed residential land development trends and capital allocation. Barbour noted no significant project delays and described a "fairly consistent pace of orders." Cottrill reiterated a balanced approach to capital deployment.
  • Trey Grooms, Stephens: Asked about SG&A management and geographic performance. Cottrill stated SG&A is targeted at 14% of revenue, with cost initiatives ongoing. Higgins and Barbour highlighted strength in the Southeast and Texas, with lower market shares providing runway for growth.
  • Collin Verron, Deutsche Bank: Sought clarity on manufacturing, transportation, and CapEx. Barbour detailed logistics improvements and cost absorption timing; Cottrill said free cash flow is targeted at greater than 40% of EBITDA, with CapEx prioritizing high-margin growth areas.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on pricing discipline, demand visibility, order trends, and capital allocation, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious sentiment due to macro uncertainty and postponed long-term guidance.
  • Management maintained confidence in strategic execution, market share gains, and profitability, though acknowledged uncertainty, especially in providing long-term projections. Barbour's tone was confident in prepared remarks but cautious during Q&A, especially on market outlook and guidance range.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a slight increase in caution from both analysts and management, driven by ongoing economic uncertainties and the decision to delay Investor Day.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fiscal 2026 revenue guidance is now $2.825 billion to $2.975 billion, compared to unchanged guidance in the prior quarter. The company postponed Investor Day, shifting from the previously announced June date, due to challenges in forecasting a three-year plan.
  • Management's tone shifted from steady confidence to a more conservative stance, citing continued economic volatility. Analysts' questions focused more on risk management, SG&A discipline, and pricing stability than in the previous quarter.
  • Strategic focus evolved to emphasize diversification, resilient profitability, and capital allocation flexibility. Key metrics such as net sales, adjusted EBITDA, and capital spending showed incremental growth year over year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Higher interest rates, economic uncertainty, and unfavorable weather conditions impacted demand, particularly in Q4. Management does not expect material tariff impacts but remains watchful of potential project delays.
  • Residential and nonresidential markets are expected to be pressured, while agriculture and international may see double-digit declines. Manufacturing costs are projected to be a headwind in the first quarter due to lower production volume, though transportation costs are expected to improve through logistics initiatives.
  • Management is cautious in forecasting and has implemented ongoing cost control and investment programs to mitigate risk.

Final Takeaway

Advanced Drainage Systems ended fiscal 2025 with resilient profitability and strong cash generation, supported by product and geographic diversification. Management expects a challenging but stable demand environment in fiscal 2026, with revenue guidance reflecting modest volume growth and pricing headwinds. Strategic investments in innovation, capacity, and acquisitions continue, while cost discipline and a conservative approach to guidance underscore the company's focus on long-term shareholder value amid economic uncertainty.

data.title: BioHarvest outlines path to $11M–$12M quarterly revenue for adjusted EBITDA breakeven while expanding VINIA portfolio and CDMO pipeline
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BioHarvest Sciences Inc. (BHST) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ilan Sobel highlighted the company's Botanical Synthesis technology and its application in producing plant-based compounds for pharmaceutical, nutraceutical, cosmetic, and nutrition markets. He reported, "Revenue rose 47% year-over-year to $7.9 million, exceeding our $7.8 million guidance, driven by balanced growth in the VINIA business across both our vinia.com website and our Amazon business." Sobel noted the milestone of surpassing 50,000 active subscribers in the USA for VINIA, with recurring subscribers making up 90% of vinia.com revenue.

  • Gross margins improved by 227 basis points to 58.5%. Sobel stated, "These significant gross profit margin gains that we have achieved over the last two years have been all through our efforts to drive scale and efficiencies in the business as we have continued to maintain the same retail price across all our capsule-related SKUs for the last four years since entering the USA on May 12, 2015."

  • Notable product expansion included the VINIA SuperFood Tea lineup, which received a 4.9 out of 5 rating on Amazon, and the upcoming launch of VINIA Daily 2X Formula Chews, designed for active consumers and athletes, certified by Informed Sport. Expansion into hydration beverages and the introduction of olive verbascoside cell (planned for 2026) were highlighted as growth drivers.

  • The CDMO services division completed Stage 1 of a major program with a NASDAQ-listed pharmaceutical partner. Sobel said, "This is a major win for our company and a sign for what is coming."

  • CFO Bar Dichter reported, "Revenue for the first quarter of 2025 increased 47% to $7.9 million, which exceed our prior revenue guidance as compared to $5.3 million in the first quarter of 2024. Gross profit increased 53% to $4.6 million or 58.5% of total revenue... Net losses for the first quarter of 2025 totaled $2.3 million or $0.13 per basic and diluted share."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated the target of reaching adjusted EBITDA breakeven at $11 million to $12 million in quarterly revenue, with expectations to achieve this in the second half of 2025.

  • Long-term, Sobel stated, "We target a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin for our direct-to-consumer Health and Wellness products division with even higher margins expected in the CDMO."

  • The second quarter guidance includes contributions from the CDMO business, with anticipated additional CDMO deals in 2025.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 2025 reached $7.9 million, up from $5.3 million in Q1 2024.

  • Gross profit increased to $4.6 million, with margins at 58.5% compared to 56.2% in the prior year.

  • Operating expenses totaled $6.3 million, up from $4.4 million in the same period last year. Net loss was $2.3 million, a notable improvement over the prior year’s $6.6 million loss.

  • Adjusted EBITDA loss was $1.4 million, and cash and cash equivalents stood at $3.4 million as of March 31, 2025. The company raised $3.9 million in debt during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Matt Hewitt, Craig-Hallum Capital Group: Asked about the CDMO contract's Stage 2 timeline and margin trajectory. Ilan Sobel explained that Stage 2 involves transitioning to liquid media and should take six to nine months, emphasizing, "Phase 1 is clearly the most challenging...and now it's a matter of just conditioning the cells and getting them used to a liquid media environment."

  • Hewitt inquired about the Tate & Lyle partnership. Sobel stated that updates on Stage 1 outcomes are expected before year-end.

  • Amit Dayal, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about product revenue contributors and future launches. Sobel outlined three product lines in Q1, expanding to five by year-end.

  • Dayal also asked about marketing cost trends and CDMO business contribution. Sobel detailed multi-channel marketing strategies and confirmed CDMO revenue is included in Q2 guidance.

  • Susan Anderson, Canaccord Genuity: Inquired about long-term product pipeline and new category launches, including beauty and skin health. Sobel described a focused approach to multi-billion-dollar categories and plans to launch a health and beauty proposition in Q1 next year.

  • Nicholas Sherwood, Maxim Group: Asked about regulatory support for CDMO partners. Sobel noted strong tailwinds and manageable regulatory pathways, supported by expert consultants.

  • Hunter Diamond, Diamond Equity Research: Requested clarity on the olive cell product ramp, to which Sobel responded that the ramp-up will be much faster than VINIA due to complementary benefits and existing subscriber base.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating management and probing expansion and margin strategies, with no notable skepticism present.

  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone, repeatedly emphasizing operational milestones and strategic progress—"I've never been more confident in our ability to execute and deliver on our North Star."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased optimism, with analysts expressing more enthusiasm for growth catalysts and management demonstrating heightened assurance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management reiterated the adjusted EBITDA breakeven target of $11 million to $12 million in quarterly revenue and continued focus on scale and margin expansion.

  • Product innovation moved forward with the launch of new VINIA SKUs and expansion into hydration and beauty categories, adding more near-term and mid-term catalysts compared to Q4 2024.

  • The CDMO division advanced with the completion of Stage 1 for a major pharmaceutical partner, while the prior quarter focused on building the pipeline and initial milestones.

  • Analysts’ questions shifted to more detailed product and margin inquiries, with less emphasis on balance sheet constraints, reflecting improved operational confidence.

  • Management's tone was more assertive on executing strategic priorities, highlighting specific timelines and market entry plans.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged increased operating expenses, particularly in marketing and CDMO division, but emphasized improved leverage and reduction in marketing spend as a percentage of revenue.

  • There was mention of ongoing investment in R&D and AI to optimize efficiency and margins.

  • No significant analyst-raised concerns about regulatory, supply chain, or competitive risks for the current period.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored BioHarvest's progress in scaling its VINIA product portfolio and CDMO pipeline, supported by robust revenue and margin growth. The company remains focused on achieving adjusted EBITDA breakeven at $11 million to $12 million in quarterly revenue in the second half of 2025, while continuing to introduce new products and expand into additional high-value categories. Strategic investments in R&D and AI, along with successful execution of CDMO contracts, are expected to drive future profitability and operational leverage.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Duos Technologies outlines path to 15 Edge Data Centers and $28M–$30M revenue target for 2025 while expanding asset management portfolio
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Duos Technologies Group, Inc. (DUOT) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Chuck Ferry reported significant progress in both the Power and Edge Data Center lines of business, highlighting the successful contracting of 570 megawatts under the Asset Management Agreement with APR Energy, up 180 megawatts in just six weeks, and projected an additional 160 megawatts to close soon, bringing the total to approximately 730 megawatts. Ferry stated, "Altogether, this means we will have approximately 730 megawatts of gas turbines contracted in just 5 months for entering into our Asset Management Agreement with APR Energy and Fortress Investment Group."
  • Ferry further emphasized customer commitment for eight additional Edge Data Centers and reaffirmed the plan to place 15 Edge Data Centers by year-end, noting, “We remain confident in our plan to place 15 Edge Data Centers by the end of this year. Overall, we are on track to execute our strategy and meet the guidance that we have previously issued.”
  • CFO Adrian Goldfarb explained the company’s new reporting structure across three segments and described the quarter as the beginning of a “wholesale transformation for Duos.” Goldfarb noted, “It is important to understand that while the results being presented are significantly improved compared to a year ago, this is just the beginning of a wholesale transformation for Duos.”
  • Goldfarb disclosed that the Edge AI division has secured financial arrangements with Accu-Tech to supply Edge Data Centers, targeting 15 deployments by year-end. He stated, “We expect to enter 2026 with more than $3 million in annual recurring revenue on multiyear contract.”
  • Goldfarb reported that the Asset Management Agreement with New APR Energy generated almost $4 million in Q1 revenues and expects this to grow over the next three quarters, supporting previously issued guidance. He added, “Total revenues for Q1 2025 increased 363% to $4.95 million compared to $1.07 million in the first quarter of 2024.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated full-year consolidated revenue guidance of $28 million to $30 million across the three subsidiaries. Goldfarb stated, “We are maintaining that guidance where we expect to record between $28 million and $30 million in consolidated revenue from our 3 subsidiaries.”
  • For Q2 2025, Goldfarb expects performance similar to Q1, aligning with the upper end of the projected range. “Our performance in Q1 was at the upper end of the projected range of $4 million to $5 million, and I expect a similar performance in Q2.”
  • The company continues to expect losses in the first half of 2025 with plans to “breakeven and may make money in the third and fourth quarters, and end the full year with positive adjusted EBITDA.”

Financial Results

  • Q1 2025 total revenues were $4.95 million, driven primarily by $4.9 million in recurring services and consulting revenue, of which $3.9 million was attributed to the Asset Management Agreement with New APR Energy.
  • Gross margin for Q1 2025 improved to $1.31 million, an increase of 1288% compared to $90,000 in Q1 2024, with more than $900,000 in revenue recognized from a 5% equity interest in New APR’s parent, contributing at 100% margin.
  • Operating expenses for the quarter increased to $3.1 million, attributed to non-cash stock-based compensation and increased R&D expenses.
  • Net operating loss narrowed to $1.79 million from $2.76 million in Q1 2024. Net loss was $2.08 million compared to $2.75 million in the prior year period.
  • The company ended the quarter with $6.48 million in cash and shareholder equity over $5.1 million. Duos also retired $1 million in debt during the quarter and expects to retire an additional $1.2 million by year-end.
  • Current contracts and backlog represent more than $45 million in revenue, with $17.4 million projected for recognition in 2025 and $7–$8 million in near-term awards and renewals.

Q&A

  • Michael Latimore, Northland Capital Markets, asked if the 32% gross margin for the Power business is a good range for the year. Ferry responded, “Yes. So on the on the Power business, we feel very confident in the forecast that we put together for the year on that. And yes, so that’s a that’s a good number to think about for our gross margin.”
  • Latimore asked about visibility and hyperscaler opportunities in the data center business. Ferry said, “It also attracted the attention of a couple of different hyperscalers that I would prefer not to disclose their names at this time but we are in active discussions with probably about 3 or 4 hyperscalers.”
  • Ed Woo, Ascendiant Capital Markets, inquired about the impact of tariffs or macro changes on the sales cycle. Ferry replied, “No. No. We haven’t. And we’re actually in pretty good shape compared to some other companies in that regard… there is no impacts to us on those 2 lines of business.”
  • Dan Weston, West Capital Management, asked about the Edge Data Center ramp and customer mix. Ferry explained it will be “a combination of both” school districts and hyperscalers, and described the potential for pod farms and distributed deployments.
  • Weston asked about the Tampa data center park project. Ferry shared, “The plans right now are going to develop that data center park. We have progressed it to the point where we will close on actually owning the property there probably in the next two months.”
  • Weston asked about power asset allocation for new projects. Ferry said, “The demand for this is so high. We are assisting APR Energy in evaluating opportunities to acquire additional assets so we can actually grow the overall value of the APR business.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and congratulatory tone, frequently commending management on progress and pressing for more detail on growth opportunities.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently expressing certainty: “we feel very confident,” “we remain confident in our plan,” and “the outlook for Duos looks very promising right now.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts’ sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to clear confidence, supported by substantial revenue and margin improvements and a more detailed strategic roadmap.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s language on guidance and strategic direction remained consistent, with an increase in specificity around Power and Edge Data Center targets and contract wins.
  • Revenue for Q1 2025 rose sharply from the seasonally lower Q4 2024, reflecting the impact of new contracts and business lines.
  • Gross margin turned positive and expanded significantly from negative levels in Q4 2024, driven by the New APR Energy agreement.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from questions on risk and execution to more detailed inquiries about growth drivers, customer types, and new project opportunities.
  • The overall tone evolved from expectation-setting to reporting early execution and tangible results.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing slow adoption in the rail industry but highlighted continued work and plans for new product rollouts.
  • Tariffs were addressed, with Ferry stating there is currently no impact on either Power or Edge Data Center businesses, though the situation will be monitored.
  • The company acknowledged that losses are still expected in the first half of the year, with a plan to minimize them through expense reductions and offset by some anticipated one-time expenses.
  • Asset utilization and maintaining high rates of deployment in the Power business were discussed as ongoing priorities.

Final Takeaway

Duos Technologies Group, Inc. management emphasized strong progress in both the Power and Edge Data Center segments, achieving substantial revenue growth and improved gross margins in Q1 2025. The company reaffirmed its $28 million to $30 million revenue target and plan for 15 Edge Data Centers by year-end, supported by a robust contract backlog and expanding customer base that now includes active discussions with hyperscalers. Management remains confident in the company’s transformation and its ability to achieve breakeven and positive adjusted EBITDA in the second half of 2025 while continuing to monitor and address risks related to rail industry adoption and macroeconomic factors like tariffs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
